The transition marks a strategic inflection point: where Cook built Apple into a $4 trillion services powerhouse, Ternus brings a 23-year track record as a hardware engineer who shaped AirPods, the Apple Watch, and Vision Pro. His appointment signals the company intends to compete in AI not through foundational model development, but through intelligent, connected hardware products.

## Hardware engineering takes the helm

Ternus joined Apple in 2001 and rose through hardware engineering ranks, contributing to some of the company's highest-margin product categories. His resume includes AirPods—which generated an estimated $40 billion in Services revenue for Apple—and the Vision Pro spatial computing device. Unlike Cook's background in operations and supply chain management, Ternus's expertise lies in device architecture and product integration.

The change arrives as Apple faces concrete competitive and operational pressures. Supply chain volatility, memory chip shortages, and tariff uncertainty under the Trump administration threaten margins. Roughly 80% of iPhones were produced in China before recent tariffs; Apple has begun diversifying to India, which now accounts for approximately 25% of iPhone production.

## Connected AI devices, not foundation models

Rather than compete directly with OpenAI, Google, or Anthropic on large language models, Ternus is expected to push Apple toward AI-enabled consumer hardware that connects to the iPhone ecosystem. Reported projects include smart glasses, AI-enhanced AirPods, wearable pendants with built-in cameras, and home robotics—a particular interest of Ternus, who studied mechanical engineering and built assistive devices for people with mobility disabilities.

Foldable iPhones, rumored for years while competitors shipped competing products, may launch under Ternus's leadership as early as September. Apple has taken a deliberately slower approach, waiting for technology maturity. Home robotics concepts are more speculative but align with Ternus's background: designs range from tabletop devices with robotic arms to mobile units that act as moving displays. Humanoid robots appear to be exploratory, likely years from commercialization.

For enterprise AI practitioners, this signals Apple's bet: the next wave of AI adoption runs through devices people already own or will carry, not through web-based applications or APIs alone. Whether Ternus can execute this vision while navigating tariff policy shifts and supply chain concentration in China will determine whether this represents strategic clarity or organizational distraction.
items.original_title: Apple under Ternus: what comes next for the tech giant’s hardware strategy

items.headline: Discord Users Breach Anthropic's Restricted Mythos AI Model
items.body:
A group of Discord users gained unauthorized access to Anthropic's Mythos Preview, a closely guarded AI tool designed to identify security vulnerabilities in software and networks. The breach highlights the gap between Anthropic's access controls and the determination of researchers willing to piece together publicly available information. The incident raises questions about how thoroughly companies restrict experimental AI tools and whether current safeguards adequately protect systems intended for limited use.

## How the Access Occurred

The Discord group exploited multiple vectors to reach Mythos. They examined data from a recent breach of Mercor, an AI training startup, and used knowledge of Anthropic's standard model naming conventions to make an educated guess about where the tool was hosted online. One member leveraged existing permissions they held through work as a contractor for an Anthropic partner firm, which granted them access to other Anthropic models. This combination of detective work and legitimate credentials gave the group entry not only to Mythos but to other unreleased Anthropic models as well.

According to Bloomberg's reporting, the group used their access cautiously, building only simple websites rather than attempting to exploit Mythos's vulnerability-detection capabilities. This restraint appears designed to avoid triggering Anthropic's monitoring systems and detection mechanisms. The breach itself was discovered and disclosed through Bloomberg's investigation rather than through Anthropic's own security monitoring.

## Implications for AI Containment Strategy

The incident demonstrates that controlling access to powerful AI systems requires more than credential management and URL obfuscation. Attackers with partial legitimate access can extrapolate system locations and configurations, particularly when companies follow consistent infrastructure patterns. For enterprises deploying restricted AI models, this suggests that predictable naming schemes and hosting conventions create exploitable patterns.

More broadly, the breach underscores a persistent tension in AI development: companies want to study dangerous capabilities in controlled environments, but restricting access while using standard infrastructure creates an incentive structure that rewards sophisticated reconnaissance. Organizations developing similar experimental tools should assume that multiple stakeholders will attempt to locate and access them, and should architect access controls accordingly rather than relying on security through obscurity. The practical takeaway for enterprises: if you're limiting access to a sensitive AI system, assume sophisticated users will map your infrastructure and use partial credentials to probe for expansion opportunities.
items.original_title: Discord Sleuths Gained Unauthorized Access to Anthropic’s Mythos
